[PMC Copyright notice](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/about/copyright/) PMCID: PMC8728049 PMID: [35024492](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/35024492/) ## Abstract There is increasing evidence from animal and human studies suggesting that fasting can play a role in disease prevention, weight control and longevity. However, few studies have compared exercise performances in individuals adhering to an intermittent fasting (IF) in comparison to individuals who are not. Given the rising popularity of IF we aim to investigate whether this type eating pattern will improve cardiovascular performance over a period of 12 weeks through VO2 max measurements in participants from a Lebanese community. Additionally, we will study the variation of different health parameters, physical performance and biomarkers potentially affected by IF. Participants will be recruited from a large university community and randomized into 4 arms. Baseline information will be collected from all participants, which includes biological, physical, nutritional, medical and psychological data. Two arms will follow a time-restricted fasting diet with and without physical exercise, one arm will exercise without fasting, and one will act as a control group. Throughout the study, measurements will be repeated, and data analysis will follow to evaluate results. **Keywords:** Intermittent fasting, Nutrition, Sports medicine, Aerobic exercise, Exercise performance, Health biomarkers, Weight loss, Maximal oxygen consumption, FATMAX, VO2max ## 1. Introduction Intermittent Fasting (IF) is an umbrella term which covers many types of dietary timing patterns, all of which revolve around establishing periods of voluntary abstinence from caloric intake during the day or the week [[1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8728049/#bib1)]. While fasting practices have been present for centuries, the IF practice is enjoying a noticeable rise in popularity in recent years [[2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8728049/#bib2)]. There is an increasing amount of evidence from animal and human studies suggesting that IF may have beneficial effects on a multitude of health parameters. Studies found that fasting decreases the risk factors of cardiovascular diseases, reduces insulin resistance, helps in weight loss among other health benefits [[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8728049/#bib3)] (see [Table 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8728049/#tbl1)) ### Table 1. SPIRIT checklist of study events. [![1.](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/4bd7/8728049/ec95e68113a0/fx1.jpg)](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/core/lw/2.0/html/tileshop_pmc/tileshop_pmc_inline.html?title=Click%20on%20image%20to%20zoom&p=PMC3&id=8728049_fx1.jpg) [Open in a new tab](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8728049/table/tbl1/) While IF showed positive effects on metabolic health outcomes, its impact on physical performance, fat oxidation rate and muscle mass is still controversial. In the body of the current literature, performance is measured using VO2max (aerobic capacity), which is considered an objective measure of cardiorespiratory fitness (CRF) and the gold standard for testing fitness performance. Athletes with high VO2max produce more energy, achieve higher intensity and performance during their workout [[4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8728049/#bib4)]. Moreover, low levels of VO2max and CRF are considered as predictors for chronic diseases leading to an increase in cardiovascular diseases morbidity and all-cause mortality, therefore maintaining a considerably high CRF level is crucial for our health [[[4]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8728049/#bib4), [[5]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8728049/#bib5), [[6]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8728049/#bib6)]. Studies found that vigorous to high intensity exercise are positively correlate with CRF [[7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8728049/#bib7)], however, studies assessing the effect IF on physical performance have conflicting evidence. The lack of methodological homogeneity makes it difficult to draw definite conclusions using the existing literature [[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8728049/#bib3)]. In fact, some studies reported that IF accompanied with endurance, high-intensity or resistance exercises didn't show any performance benefit, yet in fact some results indicated a decline in performance [[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8728049/#bib3),[[8]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8728049/#bib8), [[9]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8728049/#bib9), [[10]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8728049/#bib10)]; however, other studies demonstrated that resistance training and IF improved body strength, performance and muscular endurance [[11](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8728049/#bib11),[12](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8728049/#bib12)]. This is further supported by recent evidence [[13](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8728049/#bib13)]. In addition, enabling fat metabolism is crucial for exercise performance and health in general [[14](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8728049/#bib14)]. Studies showed that fasting for more than 6 h enhances fat oxidation [[15](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8728049/#bib15)]. The maximal fat oxidation rates (FATMAX) is achieved during submaximal exercise ( Available from: [[DOI](https://doi.org/10.1146/annurev-nutr-071816-064634)] [[PubMed](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28715993/)] [[Google Scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar_lookup?journal=Annu.%20Rev.%20Nutr.&title=Metabolic%20effects%20of%20intermittent%20fasting&author=R.E.%20Patterson&author=D.D.%20Sears&volume=37&issue=1&publication_year=2017%20Aug%2021&pages=371-393&pmid=28715993&doi=10.1146/annurev-nutr-071816-064634&)] * 3.Levy E., Chu T. Intermittent fasting and its effects on athletic performance. Curr. Sports Med. Rep. 2019 Jul;18(7):266–269. doi: 10.1249/JSR.0000000000000614. Available from: [[DOI](https://doi.org/10.1249/JSR.0000000000000614)] [[PubMed](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31283627/)] [[Google Scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar_lookup?journal=Curr.%20Sports%20Med.%20Rep.&title=Intermittent%20fasting%20and%20its%20effects%20on%20athletic%20performance&author=E.%20Levy&author=T.%20Chu&volume=18&issue=7&publication_year=2019%20Jul&pages=266-269&pmid=31283627&doi=10.1249/JSR.0000000000000614&)] * 4.Harber M.P., Kaminsky L.A., Arena R., Blair S.N., Franklin B."}, {"url": "https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-66366-8_reference.pdf", "title": "", "content": "Title: s41467-025-66366-8_reference.pdf\n\nURL Source: https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-66366-8_reference.pdf\n\nPublished Time: Sat, 13 Dec 2025 15:55:20 GMT\n\nNumber of Pages: 32\n\nMarkdown Content:\nhttps://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-025-66366-8 \n\nReceived: 3 November 2024 Accepted: 4 November 2025 \n\nCite this article as: Barve, R.A., Veronese, N., Bertozzi, B. et al. Cardiometabolic and molecular adaptations to 6-month intermittent fasting in middle-aged men and women with overweight: secondary outcomes of a randomized controlled trial. Nat Commun (2025). https:// doi.org/10.1038/s41467-025-66366-8 \n\nRuteja A. Barve, Nicola Veronese, Beatrice Bertozzi, Valeria Tosti, Maria Lastra Cagigas, Francesco Spelta, Edda Cava, Laura Piccio, Dayna S. Early, Richard D. Head & Luigi Fontana \n\nWe are providing an unedited version of this manuscript to give early access to its \n\nfindings. Before final publication, the manuscript will undergo further editing. To view a copy of this licence, visit http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/. \n\n# Nature Communications \n\n# Article in Press \n\n# Cardiometabolic and molecular adaptations to 6-month intermittent fasting in middle-aged men and women with overweight: secondary outcomes of a randomized controlled trial \n\n> ARTICLE IN PRESS\n\nCardiometabolic and molecular adaptations to 6 -month intermittent fasting in middle -aged men and \n\nwomen with overweight: secondary outcomes of a randomized controlled trial \n\nRuteja A Barve 1*, Nicola Veronese 2* , Beatrice Bertozzi 3* , Valeria Tosti 3, Maria L astra Cagigas 4, Francesco \n\nSpelta 5, Edda Cava 6, Laura Piccio 4,7 , Dayna S Early 3, Richard D Head 1, Luigi Fontana 4,8\n\n> 1\n\nDepartment of Genetics, Washington University, St. Louis, MO, USA \n\n> 2\n\nGeriatric Unit, Department of Internal Medicine and Geriatrics, University of Palermo, Palermo, Italy \n\n> 3\n\nDepartment of Medicine, Washington University, St. Louis, MO, USA \n\n> 4\n\nCharles Perkins Center, University of Sydney, Sydney, NSW, Australia \n\n> 5\n\nGeriatric Unit , AULSS 9 Scaligera, “Mater Salutis” Hospital, Legnago, Verona, Italy \n\n> 6\n\nUnit of Dietetic and Clinical Nutrition, San Camillo, Forlanini Hospital, Rome, Italy \n\n> 7\n\nBrain and Mind Centre, School of Medical Sciences, University of Sydney, Sydney, NSW, Australia \n\n> 8\n\nDepartment of Endocrinology, Royal Prince Alfred Hospital, Sydney, NSW, Australia. \n\n* These authors contributed equally \n\nCorresponding author: Luigi Fontana, MD, PhD, FRACP  luigi.fontana@sydney.edu.au  (or \n\n@LuigiFontanaMD on Twitter; ORCID 0000 -0001 -8500 -5537) \n\n> ARTICLE IN PRESS\n\nARTICLE IN PRESS ABSTRACT \n\nIntermittent fasting (IF) has gained attention as a potential intervention for cardiometabolic health, \n\nthough its long -term effects remain unclear . In this  randomized clinical trial , we assessed the impact of 6\n\nmonths of IF on body composition, cardiovascular risk factors, and related molecular pathways in middle -\n\naged (30 -65 years) men and women with  overweight (BMI 24.8 –35 kg/m²). In this trial, 41 participants \n\nwere randomized to either an intermittent fasting (IF) intervention or to maintain their habitual diet. The \n\nprimary outcome (circulating CRP concentration ) was previously reported; here, we present exploratory \n\nanalyses focusing on metabolomic and transcriptomic responses. IF led to an 8% reduction in body weight ,\n\na 16% decrease in body fat, and significant improvements in lipid profile, including substantial reductions \n\nin plasma LDL -cholesterol, non -HDL -cholesterol, and triglycerides (p=0.001). However, no significant \n\nchanges were observed in other cardiometabolic risk factors.  To investigate th e underlying molecular \n\nmechanisms , we performed untargeted plasma metabolomics and transcriptomic analysis of colon \n\nmucosa biopsies. Significant multi -omic changes were identified, particularly in lipid metabolism, bile acid \n\nsignaling, and enteroendocrine regulation . Notably, there was a downregulation of transcripts related to \n\nglucagon -like peptide 1 (GLP -1) and related enteroendocrine hormones . Correlation analysis highlighted \n\nkey molecular pathways, with PPAR -α and B -cell -mediated immune processes significantly associated with \n\nchanges in non -HDL cholesterol. Our findings extend the understanding of IF in humans beyond weight \n\nloss, providing key mechanistic insights to inform targeted therapies for improving cardiometabolic \n\nhealth. ClinicalTrials.gov NCT01964118 \n\nINTRODUCTION \n\nCardiovascular disease remains a global health concern, contributing to substantial morbidity, disability, \n\nmortality , and escalating healthcare costs .(1) Men and women with optimal cardiometabolic health at age \n\n50 have a 13 -fold lower risk of developing cardiovascular disease compared to those with two or more \n\nab normal risk factors, even accounting for longer life expectancy .(2) Previous research, both \n\nobservational and randomized trials, has demonstrated that daily calorie restriction with a balanced \n\nintake of nutrient -rich foods, particularly those high in vegetable fiber and vitamins, causes substantial \n\nimprovements in multiple major cardiometabolic risk factors. (3, 4) These effects have been observed even \n\nin lean and slightly overweight young adults. (5) However, adhering to daily calorie restriction can be \n\nchallenging, (6) highlighting the need for easier -to -implement strategies. Intermittent fasting (IF) has \n\ngained popular ity for weight loss, allowing individuals to follow their regular diet on non -fasting days .(7)\n\nHowever, despite promising results in rodents, clinical trials on IF in adults with overweight and obes ity \n\n> ARTICLE IN PRESS\n\nARTICLE IN PRESS have shown mixed effects on cardiometabolic indicators, (7-9) with one trial even reporting increased \n\nplasma LDL -cholesterol levels. (10 )\n\nThe present randomized clinical trial was designed to assess whether sustained weight loss achieved \n\nthrough long -term IF can replicate the cardiometabolic benefi ts observed with chronic calorie restriction \n\nwith optimal nutrient intake in men and women with overweight and obes ity , while also exploring the \n\nunderlying molecular mechanisms . The primary outcome (circulating CRP concentration) was previously \n\nreported , with no significant changes in CRP.(34) H ere, we present secondary analyses focusing on \n\nmetabolomic and transcriptomic responses.  A key aspect of this trial is the multi -omic analysis of colon \n\nmucosa biopsies, aimed at understanding how IF impacts lipid me tabolism in the colon and \n\ncardiometabolic health. The colon plays a critical role in nutrient absorption, cholesterol metabolism, and \n\nbile acid recycling –key processes in maintaining metabolic homeostasis. (11 ) Molecular analysis of colon \n\ntissue can elucidate how IF modulates lipid absorption, bile acid signaling, and inflammatory responses, \n\nall of which are essential for regulating chole sterol, triglyceride s, and overall cardiovascular health. (12 )\n\nAdditionally, the colon’s interaction with the gut microbiota and its involvement in systemic inflammation \n\nand immune modulation make it a critical site for understanding the broader metabolic effects of IF .(13 ,\n\n14 ) By uncovering these mechanisms, this study aims to provide insights into how IF influences \n\ncardiometabolic outcomes, potentially informing targeted dietary, pharmacological, and personalized \n\nmetabolic strategies .\n\nIn this stu dy, we show that six months of IF significantly reduces body weight and adiposity, leading to \n\nfavourable changes in lipid profiles, but without affecting other major cardiovascular risk factors. Notably, \n\nbaseline levels of PPAR -α–related transcripts predicted the magnitude of lipid improvements. Integrated \n\nmulti -omic profiling of plasma and colonic mucosa revealed downregulation of GLP -1–associated \n\nenteroendocrine signalling, including reduced glucagon (GCG) and peptide YY (PYY) expression, alongside \n\nmodulation of B -cell pathways. These findings extend current understanding of IF beyond weight loss, \n\nproviding mechanistic insights into its cardiometabolic benefits. \n\nRESULTS \n\nStudy design . The study design is outlined in the Methods . Out of the 66 participants initially assessed for \n\neligibility, 50 were randomized, and 49 began the intervention ( Supplementary Figure 1 ). In the initial 6 -\n\nmonth phase, 75% (n=21) of participants in the IF group (Group A) and 9 1% (n=20) in the control group \n\n(Group B) completed the study. Following this phase, 18 of the 21 participants who completed Phase 1 in \n\nGroup A continued the IF intervention for an additional 6 months (Group C), while 19 of the 20 participants \n\nfrom Group B who completed Phase 1 crossed over to IF for the second 6 -month period (Group D). \n\n> ARTICLE IN PRESS\n\nARTICLE IN PRESS Retention rates during this second phase were strong , with dropout rates of 16.6% (n=3) in Group C \n\n(participants who continued fasting for an additional 6 months) and 0% (n=0 ) in Group D (participants \n\nwho transitioned from the control group to IF in the second phase). As illustrated in Supplementary Figure \n\n2, principal component analysis (PCA) shows a near -complete overlap of all measurements listed in Table \n\n1 for Groups A and D. Consequently, these two independent 6 -month fasting groups were combined \n\n(referred to as IF -combined or Group A+D) in Tables 1 –3.\n\nIntermittent fasting leads to a significant reduction in body weight and adiposity \n\nParticipants, who on average presented overweight at baseline ( mean BMI 29.6±2.7 kg/m 2), experienced \n\nsignificant weight loss after 6 months of IF (p<0.0001) . As shown in Table 1, t he IF Group A experienced a \n\nreduction of -5.9±3.6 kg, while the IF -combined Group A+D saw a loss of -7.0±3.5 kg. Conversely, the \n\ncontrol Group B exhibited a slight weight gain of +1.1±3.2 kg (p=0.17) . These changes corresponded to -\n\n7%, -8%, and +1% of baseline body weights, respectively. Significant decreases in waist circumference \n\nwere observed in the IF Group A ( -8.4±7.8 cm ; p<0.0001 ), the IF -combined Group A+D ( -7.7±6.6 cm ;\n\np<0.0001 ), while the control Group B experienced a slight non -significant waistline gain of +1.1±6.9 cm \n\n(Table 1). Additionally, all IF groups showed reductions in whole -body fat , trunk fat, and lean mass \n\ncompared to controls (p<0.0001) . Self -reported energy intake significantly decreased in all IF groups, with \n\nan average weekly calorie restriction of 22.8% achieved over the 6 months . These findings hig hlight the \n\nsignificant improvements in weight, body composition, and calorie intake achieved through IF .\n\nIntermittent Fasting Induces Favorable Changes in Lipid Profile \n\nTable 2 highlights significant changes induced by IF in total cholesterol , LDL -C and non -HDL -C. Notably, \n\nnon -HDL -C experienced the most significant reduction, with an 11 % decrease from baseline in the IF -\n\ncombined group (P<0.001) , contrasting with a significant increase observed in the control Group B \n\n(p=0.03) . Although HDL -C levels increased in both IF Group A and IF -Combined Group A+D, these increases \n\ndid not reach statistical significance. However , a significant decrease in the total cholesterol to HDL -C ratio \n\nwas observed in both IF groups (p<0.001) . Remarkably, p lasma TG concentrations markedly decreased in \n\nboth IF Group A and IF -Combined Group A+D by 19.4% and 23.4%, respectively (p<0.007) . Nonetheless ,\n\nthe triglyceride to HDL -C ratio, a proxy measure of insulin resistance, was significantly reduced only in IF -\n\nCombined Gr oup A+D (p=0.001) .\n\nIntermittent Fasting Does Not Reduce Other Major Cardiovascular Risk Factors \n\n> ARTICLE IN PRESS\n\nARTICLE IN PRESS Despite notable reductions in body weight, waist circumference, and trunk fat, IF did no t affect fasting \n\nglucose, insulin, HOMA -IR, and hsCRP levels ( Table 3). Similarly, no changes were observed in systolic and \n\ndiastolic blood pressures. Additionally, both mean and maximum intima -media thickness of the common \n\ncarotid arteries remained unchanged in both IF Group A and IF -Combined Group A+D ( Table 3). \n\nMulti -omic changes in response to IF \n\nTo investigate the molecular impact of IF on cardiometabolic health, we conducted gene expression \n\nprofiling (mRNA sequencing) of colorectal mucosa biopsies and untargeted plasma metabolomics in 27 \n\nparticipants (14 Group A + 13 Group D) who underwent 6 months of IF . Metabolomics analysis was \n\nexploratory and therefore not a protocol pre -specified outcome . Integrated m ulti -omic data were \n\ncompared between baseline and 6 months (Supplementary Figure 3 ), with significant changes identified \n\nusing a non -parametric Wilcoxon paired test (p<0.05) and a ≥1.1 -fold change for genes. After 6 months of \n\nIF, 369 entities were upregulated , including 359 genes and 9 metabolites (benzyl alcohol, ornithine, 7 \n\nunannotated metabolites , and the protein TNF -alpha ). In contrast, 393 enti ties were downregulated ,\n\nincluding 387 genes and 6 metabolites (lactulose, 2-hydroxybutanoic acid , 3 unannotated metabolites ,\n\nand the protein IL -6). The biological knowledge analysis of significantly changed entities was conducted \n\nusing CompBio v2.6, resulting in two distinct maps representing biological processes – one for upregulated \n\nprocesses and another for downregulated processes. No biological themes met the significance threshold \n\nfor upregulated entit ies (Source Data ). In contrast, more than 30 interconnected biological themes were \n\nsignificant for downregulated entities . Full maps, along with a comprehensive list of themes, associated \n\ngenes, and concepts, are available in the supplementary material (Supplementary Figure 4 ). Fig ure 1\n\nhighlights the key findings related to the downregulated entities associated with 6 -month IF . Notably, \n\nenteroendocrine signaling emerge d as a central theme, connecting various sub -themes such as the \n\nsynthesis and secretion of glucagon -like peptide 1 ( GLP -1), a hormone crucial for appetite regulati on and \n\ninsulin secretion. Genes most strongly enriched in this theme , such as glucagon (GCG ) and peptide YY \n\n(PYY ), are highlighted in the red panel ( Fig ure 1). Additionally, the metabolite lactulose was mapped to \n\nthis theme, showing a modest enrichment score of 7.1 1.\n\nAnother significant ly downregulated theme , Pituitary Adenylate Cyclase -Activating Polypeptide (PACAP )\n\nreceptor activity , shown in blue (Figure 1 ), was strong ly inter connected with the GLP -1 theme . Key genes \n\nwithin this theme, such as vasoactive intestinal peptide ( VI P) and adenylate cyclase activating polypeptide \n\n1 (ADCYAP 1), exhibit ed the greatest enrichment scores . The two themes share d 20 genes, many of which \n\nare involved in the synthesis, secretion, and proteolytic cleavage of endocrine peptides. These genes \n\n> ARTICLE IN PRESS\n\nARTICLE IN PRESS include not only PYY and GCG , but also pancreatic polypeptide ( PPY ), somatostatin ( SST ), and others like \n\nISL LIM homeobox 1 ( ISL1 ), carboxypeptidase E ( CPE ), and secretagogin ( SCGN ). Shared genes with the \n\ngreatest contributions are visualized in the map. Figure 1 also presents a concept map for the GLP -1\n\ntheme , illustrating the interconnected terms and their relationships . On one side, terms related to \n\nappetite, hunger, and satiety are linked to GLP -1, while insulin and islet function are positioned on the \n\nopposite side, with GLP -1 at the center . Fig ure 1b (panel below the map) provides a schematic diagram \n\nthat depicts the relation ship between IF and enteroendocrine function, as revealed by the multi -omics \n\nanalysis. \n\nCorrelat ion analysis between multi -omic data and lipid /lipoprotein changes \n\nStatistically significant changes in multiple lipid and lipoprotein parameters were observed between \n\nbaseline and 6 months , as detailed in Table 2. To explore the molecular pathways potentially linked to \n\nthese lipid -related changes, we performed correlation analysis between changes in entity \n\nexpression/abundance and non -HDL cholesterol (non -HDL -C) levels over the 6 -month IF period. Using \n\nnon -parametri c Spearma n correlation analysis (r>=0.4 with p -value<1.0E -9), we identified 2 80 entities \n\npositively correlated and 32 9 entities negatively correlated with plasma non -HDL -C levels . The CompB io\n\nknowledge map for entities positively correlated with non -HDL -C (Figure 2) revealed a broad range of \n\ninterconnected processes , including lipid metabolism regulation by PPAR alp ha (PPAR -α) , protein \n\nlipidation in autophagosome assembly, brush border -enterocyte transports and unmethylated CpG \n\nbinding. Genes showing the greatest enrichment scores within these themes include d peroxisome \n\nproliferator activated receptor PPAR -α (r=0.55, p=2.22E -14) , sequestome1 SQSTM1 (r=0.41, p=3.16E -10) ,\n\nmyosin 7b MYO7B (r=0.46, p=1.08E -11) , and methyl CpG binding domain1 MBD1 (r=0.41, p=2.89E -10) .\n\nIn contrast, t he CompBio knowledge map for entities negatively correlated with non -HDL -C (Fig ure 3)\n\nunveiled a significant cluster of seventeen B -cell and immune -based themes , including isotype switching, \n\ndendritic cells in T h1 and Th2 regulation , and CCR5 chemokine receptor binding. Highly enriched genes \n\nwithin these theme s includ ed CD79a (r= -0.51 p= 3.05E -13) , Bruton tyrosine kinase BTK (r= -0.52, p1.35E -\n\n13) , complement receptor CR1 (r= -0.4,p= 4.55E -10) , Fc epsilon receptor 1 FCER2 (r= -0.42,p= 9.89E -11) ,\n\nimmunoglobulin heavy constant alpha1 IGHA1 (r= -0.43,p=6.37E -11) and immunoglobulin heavy constant \n\ngamma 1 IGHG1 (r= -0.41, p=2.02E -10) . Full maps, along with a complete list of the identified biological \n\nthemes and associated genes, are provided (Source Data , Supplementary Fig ure 5 and 6). Further analysis \n\nof the relationship between B -cell gene expression and changes in non -HDL -C revealed interesting \n\ndynamics . A median B-cell profile , constructed from seven marker genes (i.e., CD23, CD79A, CD79B , CD19 ,\n\n> ARTICLE IN PRESS\n\nARTICLE IN PRESS NUGGC , BTK and CD27 ), showed a negative correlation with the non -HDL -C ratio (r= -0.56, p=0.0021) . This \n\nsuggests that as the non -HDL -C ratio decreases during IF , the B-cell profile tend s to increase for most \n\nparticipants (Figure 3). However, a small subset of individuals exhibited an inverse correlation, suggesting \n\ndifferential immune responses to lipid changes induced by IF. Interestingly, maps generated for \n\ntriglyceride changes exhibited similar themes to those observed for non -HDL -C, though these did not \n\nreach statistical significance. As a result, all comparisons were made using non -HDL -C as the primary lipid \n\nparameter of interest. \n\nEffect of baseline profile on IF -induced m ulti -omic changes in non -HDL cholesterol \n\nVariability in non -HDL -C level changes was observed among participants over the 6 -month IF intervention \n\n(Supplementary F ig ure 7). To explore the molecular basis of this variability , we correlated baseline gene \n\nexpression with changes in non -HDL -C levels (6 -month to baseline ratio). Using non -parametric Spearman \n\ncorrelation analysis (r>=0.4 with p -value<1.0E -9), we identified 347 genes positively correlated and 535 \n\nnegatively correlated with non -HDL -C. These genes underwent analysis using CompBio v2.6 for biological \n\nknowledge mapping , revealing significant insights , particularly in the negatively correlated map. Three \n\nmain clusters emerged , linked to adherens junction s and signaling , PKA signaling and cargo transport , and \n\nepigenetic modulation (Figure 4). Full maps, along with a comprehensive list of identified biological \n\nthemes and associated genes, are available (Source Data , Supplementary Figure 8 ). At the core of these \n\nclusters is the r egulation of lipid metabolism by the PPAR -α theme , comp rising 42 entities and 32 \n\nconcepts . Within each cluster, graph panels highlight genes with the strongest enrichment for \n\nrepresentative themes . As expected , t he PPAR -α theme demonstrated strong enrichment for PPAR -α (r=-\n\n0.56, p=1.13E -14) and UCP 3 (r=-0.41, p=2.76E -10) . Given the robust correlation between baseline PPAR -\n\nα expression and non -HDL cholesterol changes , we performed a more detailed investigation into \n\nindividual pa rticipant -level alterations . Fig ure 4 shows the relationship between each participant’s \n\nbaseline PPAR -α expression (log2 CPM) and their corresponding non -HDL -C ratio (6 -month to baseline) .\n\nRemark ably , a s baseline PPAR -α expression increase d during IF , the non -HDL cholesterol ratio decrease d, \n\nindicating a reduction in non -HDL -C levels over the 6 months compared to baseline. The summary of \n\nfindings is presented in Fig ure 5 .\n\nDiscussion \n\nThis study aimed to investigate the effects of IF -induced weight loss on cardio metabolic health and its \n\nunderlying molecular mechanisms , particularly with in tissue s involved in incretin and lipid metabolism , as \n\n> ARTICLE IN PRESS\n\nARTICLE IN PRESS well as immune modulation . Our findings show that an 8% reduction in body weight after 6 months of IF \n\nis associated with significant decreases in plasma LDL -C, non -HDL -C, and TG levels . The intervention , which \n\ninvolved 2-3 non -consecutive days of vegetable -only fasting each week , was well tolerate d and \n\nsuccessfully implemented . However, IF did not significantly affect blood pressure, fasting glucose, insulin \n\nresistance (HOMA -IR), inflammat ory markers, or carotid intima -media thickness at the 6 -month time \n\npoint . Furthermore, our exploratory multi -omics analysis un covered significant connections between IF \n\nand metabolic and molecular pathways related to gut function and lipid metabolism. Specifically, IF \n\nappears to influence cellular pathways involving GLP -1 and PPAR -α function, and B-cell biology .\n\nDyslipidemia stands as the leading causal risk factor for atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. A wealth \n\nof scientific evidence from epidemiological and randomized clinical trials supports the notion that \n\nlowering LDL -C levels and triglyceride -rich lipoproteins, both recognized as atherogenic, can effectively \n\nalter the course of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease, leading to a reduction in cardiovascular \n\nevents. (15 -17 ) In our study, we observed significant IF -induced reductions in both total and LDL \n\ncholesterol. Particularly noteworthy was the marked effect on plasma non -HDL -C and TG concentrations ,\n\nwhich decreased by 11% and 25%, respectively, after just 6 months of IF, despite participants maintaining \n\na typical Western diet during non -fasting days .\n\nPPAR -α, a nuclear receptor highly expressed in the liver and intestine, regulates lipid metabolism by \n\npromoting fatty acid u ptake, utilization, and beta -oxidation during energy deprivation, essential for \n\nfasting -induced ketogenesis .(18 ) Interestingly , in our study , baseline PPAR -α expression and genes \n\ninvolved in this pathway were strongly correlated with improvements in non -HDL -C levels following IF .\n\nHigher baseline PPAR -α expression correlated with greater reduction s in non -HDL -c after 6 months of \n\nfasting. Additionally, microbial short -chain fatty acids, particularly propionate, can enhance PPAR -α\n\nactivity in intestinal cells, (19 , 20 ) potentially contributing to the observed reduction in non -HDL -C in our \n\nstudy .\n\nCholesterol metabolism plays a key role in B -cell function by promoting an anti -inflammatory response \n\nand stimulating IL -10 production .(21 ) In our study, a significant super -cluster of B -cell and immune -relate d\n\ntheme was inversely correlated with non -HDL -C, highlighting marker"}, {"url": "https://ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC9650338/", "title": "Intermittent fasting and time-restricted eating role in dietary interventions and precision nutrition", "content": "[ Skip to main content ](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9650338/#main-content)\n![](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/pd-medc-pmc-cloudpmc-viewer/production/a2b04810/var/data/static/img/us_flag.svg)\nAn official website of the United States government \nHere's how you know\nHere's how you know\n![](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/pd-medc-pmc-cloudpmc-viewer/production/a2b04810/var/data/static/img/icon-dot-gov.svg)\n**Official websites use .gov**   \nA **.gov** website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. \n![](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/pd-medc-pmc-cloudpmc-viewer/production/a2b04810/var/data/static/img/icon-https.svg)\n**Secure .gov websites use HTTPS**   \nA **lock** (  Lock Locked padlock icon  ) or **https://** means you've safely connected to the .gov website. Karras, Aristotle University of Thessaloniki, Greece; Amrendra Mishra, University of Southern California, United States\n✉\n*Correspondence: Ghada A. Soliman ghada.soliman@sph.cuny.edu\nThis article was submitted to Public Health and Nutrition, a section of the journal Frontiers in Public Health\nReceived 2022 Aug 11; Accepted 2022 Oct 10; Collection date 2022.\nCopyright © 2022 Soliman.\nThis is an open-access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution License (CC BY). The use, distribution or reproduction in other forums is permitted, provided the original author(s) and the copyright owner(s) are credited and that the original publication in this journal is cited, in accordance with accepted academic practice. No use, distribution or reproduction is permitted which does not comply with these terms.\n[PMC Copyright notice](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/about/copyright/)\nPMCID: PMC9650338 PMID: [36388372](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/36388372/)\n##  Abstract\nIntermittent fasting (IF), time-restricted eating (TRE) and fasting-mimicking diets (FMD) are gaining popularity as weight loss programs. As such, the timing and frequency of meals have been recognized as essential contributors to improving cardiometabolic health and a role as adjuvant therapy in cancer. Randomized controlled trials suggested that the weight loss associated with IF is due to a reduced energy intake due to time restriction. Although the supervised TRE clinical trials documented the dietary caloric intake, many free-living studies focused on the timing of meals without a complete characterization of the dietary intake, caloric density, or macronutrient composition. It is possible that both caloric-restriction diets and time-restriction protocols could work synergistically or additively to improve metabolic health outcomes. Like personalized medicine, achieving precision nutrition mandates the provision of the right nutrients to the right patient at the right time. To accomplish this goal, future studies need to evaluate the benefits of IF and TRE. Randomized controlled trials were conducted in different populations, ethnic groups, ages, geographic distribution, physical activity levels, body composition and in patients with obesity, diabetes, and cardiovascular diseases. Also, it is crucial to analyze the dietary composition and caloric density as related to circadian rhythm and timing of meals. It is conceivable that IF and TRE may contribute to precision nutrition strategies to achieve optimal health. However, more research is needed to evaluate IF and TRE effects on health outcomes and any side effects.\n**Keywords:** intermittent fasting (IF), time-restricted eating (TRE), fasting-mimicking diets (FMD), precision nutrition, alternative day modified fasting (ADMF)\n##  Introduction\nFasting is defined as voluntary abstinence from food and drinks for a specific period of time ([1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9650338/#B1)). Metabolically, fasting could be categorized into a post-absorptive state, fasting and starvation. In the post-absorptive state, where no glucose or other nutrients are ingested, glycogenolysis, which is the breakdown of glycogen in the liver, provides glucose to the blood and tissues. Approximately after 18–24 h. Without food, the hepatic glycogen is depleted, and gluconeogenesis, the synthesis of glucose from non-carbohydrates sources such as fat, lactate and eventually amino acids, occurs, leading to the _de novo_ synthesis of glucose for energy production. It has been suggested that fasting has a multitude of health benefits [reviewed in ([1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9650338/#B1), [2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9650338/#B2))]. Therefore, various forms of intermittent fasting and time-restricted eating have been developed over the last decade.\nThis perspective summarizes some of the current protocols in fast-mimicking diets. Intermittent fasting (IF) and time-restricted eating (TRE) refer to predetermined timing in pausing or abstaining from eating followed by ingestion of food ([3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9650338/#B3), [4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9650338/#B4)). In this approach, macronutrients providing calories are consumed in a time-specific window. Most free-living clinical studies do not restrict caloric consumption at the time-restricted window, which is considered an advantage to improving adherence as no calorie count is needed. On the other hand, traditional comprehensive weight management programs encompass caloric restriction, either as a low-calorie diet (LCD) or a very low-calorie diet (VLCD) under particular circumstances ([5](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9650338/#B5), [6](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9650338/#B6)), in addition to physical activity and behavior modification ([7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9650338/#B7)).\nFurthermore, if co-morbidities exist or at a specific Body Mass Index (BMI), pharmacological drugs and weight loss surgery may be added to the low-calorie diet, physical activity, and behavior modification protocols ([8](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9650338/#B8)–[11](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9650338/#B11)). However, the timing and frequency of meals have been recognized as essential contributors to improving cardiometabolic health, weight loss, and other health benefits, which will be discussed in the following paragraphs.\n##  Intermittent fasting\nRefers to a dietary strategy that alternates feeding days with fasting days ([12](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9650338/#B12)). The most common methods are alternating days of fasting (ADF) with one-day feasting and 1-day fasting or a 5:2 diet with 5 days of feasting and 2 days of fasting (either two consecutive fasting days or two separate days within the week). IF protocols do not restrict feeding during the feasting days, where people are allowed to eat normally or _ad libitum_ and then abstain from eating for one or 2 days ([Figure 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9650338/#F1)); however, some protocols allow for up to 500 kcal per day on fasting days ([13](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9650338/#B13)).\n### Figure 1.\n[![Figure 1](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/e5da/9650338/ee72d9a650e7/fpubh-10-1017254-g0001.jpg)](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/core/lw/2.0/html/tileshop_pmc/tileshop_pmc_inline.html?title=Click%20on%20image%20to%20zoom&p=PMC3&id=9650338_fpubh-10-1017254-g0001.jpg)\n[Open in a new tab](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9650338/figure/F1/)\nCommon caloric-restriction and time-restriction diet protocols. Comprehensive weight management programs include a low-calorie diet (LCD), physical activity and behavior modifications. In addition, pharmacological drugs or weight loss (bariatric surgery) may be added if co-morbidities exist or at a certain high BMI. LCD could be either a low carbohydrate (CHO) diet, low fat (FAT), or under specific circumstances, low protein (Prot.). A very low-calorie diet (VLCD), which provides only 800 kcal/day, is used only with medical supervision. Time-restricted diets include Intermittent fasting (IF), Time-restricted eating (TRE), and Fasting-mimicking diets (FMN). TRE could be either at the earlier part of the day, starting from 8 AM to 5 PM, or midday with delayed breakfast from 10 AM to 7 PM, or late from 12 PM−8 PM or a variation of these times as a personal preference.\n##  Time-restricted eating\nRefers to restricting the time when meals are consumed within 24 h. Most TRE studies to date ranged from 4–12 h of eating window without caloric restriction. Some investigators consider TRE a chrono-nutritional strategy ([14](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9650338/#B14), [15](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9650338/#B15)). The flexibility of the TRE protocols allows for maintaining individual eating pattern preferences, which may facilitate adherence to and compliance with the diet protocol. In addition, alternating feeding and fasting cycles following the circadian rhythm, i.e., eating during the active phase and fasting during the resting phase, may also have a favorable effect on nutrient metabolism, hormonal regulation and physiological processes and thereby may improve cardiometabolic health.\n##  Fasting-mimicking diets\nFMD differs from the IF in that it allows for reduced calorie intake instead of complete abstinence of food during the fasting period FMD constitutes periodic cycles of consecutive days consuming a reduced-calorie diet followed by eating _ad libitum_ ([15](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9650338/#B15), [16](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9650338/#B16)). FMD could be a plant-based diet, low in protein and sugar content. FMD has emerged as another dietary modification that could be beneficial in cardiometabolic diseases and weight loss programs ([16](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9650338/#B16)–[18](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9650338/#B18)), as well as an adjuvant intervention in addition to cancer therapy ([19](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9650338/#B19)–[22](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9650338/#B22)). Since FMD does not include restricting eating for a specific period of time but instead just reduced caloric intake, it will not be discussed further in this report.\nProtocols for IF and TRE are gaining popularity as weight loss programs, health benefits, and interventions in chronic diseases ([Figure 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9650338/#F1)). They are suggested to help individuals achieve a clinically significant weight loss (>5% from baseline) ([23](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9650338/#B23)), which would be relevant to improving cardiometabolic health biomarkers ([24](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9650338/#B24), [25](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9650338/#B25)). Some scholars consider IF and TRE different strategies ([14](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9650338/#B14)). As such, TRE focuses on the timing of meals and their relation to circadian rhythm, hormonal, and metabolite profile within 24 h period, while IF does not affect the chronobiology as it does not restrict feeding during the feasting days ([14](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9650338/#B14), [26](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9650338/#B26), [27](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9650338/#B27)).\nThere are also religious, ethical, and spiritual practices of fasting. For example, there are fasting practices in Judaism and Christianity. For example, fasting (abstaining from food) on Yom Kippur includes a full day from the previous day to the following evening; the orthodox Jews also abstain from drinking water for approximately 25 h ([28](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9650338/#B28)). The literature also describes Ramadan fasting for practicing Muslims as an intermittent fasting strategy for weight loss; however, fasting in Ramadan is different than intermittent fasting as it requires a consecutive 29–30 days of fasting from sunrise to sunset with nothing per oral (NPO), including both food and beverages while IF, alternative day fasting, and 5/2 strategies allow for drinking water or zero calorie beverages or black coffee alternative during the fasting day (0–500 kcal per day). When designing diet strategies, however, it is crucial to consider the feasibility, adherence, and sustainability over time. If these strategies interfere with socialization, family time, dining on special occasions, cultural, ethnic, religious, or social eating patterns, they might not be sustainable or tolerable. These factors could affect weight loss maintenance to sustain the weight loss. Incorporating behavior modification and cognitive behavioral therapy strategies such as goal setting, self-monitoring, problem-solving, and portion size control are also encouraged as adjuvants to TRE, IF, and FMD ([29](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9650338/#B29)–[31](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9650338/#B31)).\n##  Types of studies of intermittent fasting and time-restricted eating diets\n### Preclinical studies\n#### Intermittent fasting\nAnimal models have been used as a model for glucose management in diabetes [reviewed in ([28](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9650338/#B28))]. For example, Tikoo et al. ([32](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9650338/#B32)) reported that alternative day fasting did not affect the body weight in Sprague-Dawley rats compared to the control but improved the laboratory values of creatinine, albumin, and albumin improved the elevated blood pressure, and reduced type 1 diabetes induced nephropathy. In mice, Liu and colleagues reported that alternative-day fasting improved glucose tolerance, restored the pancreatic islet of Langerhans autophagy response, and enhanced beta cell survival ([33](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9650338/#B33)).\n#### Time-restricted eating\n_In vitro_ studies showed that periodic fasting might protect from oxidative damage, decreasing tumor growth and proliferation ([34](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9650338/#B34), [35](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9650338/#B35)). In animal models, TRE was investigated in mice where food availability was regulated during the 24 h circadian rhythm. The mice gained less weight, had a better blood glucose profile, longer life span, healthier muscle, and better cardiometabolic health when food was available only during a shorter window compared to the energy-matched mice fed ad-lib throughout the day ([36](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9650338/#B36)–[38](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9650338/#B38)). Mitchel and colleagues conducted a study in genetically homogenous male [_n_ = 292 C57BL/6J (B6) mice comparing feeding ad libitum (AL) to either a 30% calorie restriction diet (CR) or single-meal feeding diet (MF)]. In the MF diet (caloric intake matched to the AL diet), the mice consumed the meal quickly, inducing self-imposed fasting (21 h), which led to significant improvement in health and decreased mortality rate as evidenced by the Kaplan-Meier survival analysis. Using a multiple comparison procedure, the main lifespan extension was 11% in MF and 28% in the CR group compared to the AL diet ([39](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9650338/#B39)).\nIt is important to note that mice are nocturnal animals, and active eating time is at night. Similarly, studies in Drosophila showed that caloric restriction increased the lifespan and decreased age-related deterioration in cardiac function ([40](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9650338/#B40)). The underpinning mechanisms of these findings involved several signaling pathways. Such pathways include mTOR (mechanistic Target of Rapamycin), which regulates autophagy (auto cell degradation for recycling of nutrients) and energy metabolic pathways ([41](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9650338/#B41)), also AMPK (adenosine monophosphate kinase) and the circadian clock downstream targets.\nFurthermore, mice fed a high-fat diet restricted to the active phase (in the dark) reduced fat mass and body weight and improved glycemic index compared to mice fed the same calories _ad libitum_ ([38](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9650338/#B38)).\nTaken together, Findings from both IF and TRE suggest a beneficial effect of time-restricted feeding in murine models. However, the limitations in the translation of animal studies to humans lay in their different feeding patterns, where mice are nocturnal feeders. In contrast, human eats mainly during the day and under photopic light. Also, the duration of studies in animal models and humans varies significantly. Nevertheless, the animal model studies provided a proof-of-concept to be confirmed in human studies where the caloric intake was matched in Time-restricted feedings compared to the control subject fed ad lib ([42](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9650338/#B42), [43](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9650338/#B43)).\n### Clinical studies\n#### Intermittent fasting\nRandomized controlled trials in human suggested that the weight loss associated with intermittent fasting are due to a reduction in calories and energy intake range between 10–30% compared to baseline ([44](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9650338/#B44)–[46](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9650338/#B46)). It has been shown to effectively achieve clinically significant weight loss (i.e., reduction of 5% body weight) in the short term. Strategies for intermittent fasting include a period of fasting alternating with feeding. Alternate Day Fasting (ADF) includes fasting for 1 day (0–500 kcal/day), followed by _ad libitum_ eating for a day ([Figure 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9650338/#F1)). During the fasting days, people could consume water or zero-calorie beverages. The other common type is the 5:2 diet which includes 5 days of eating _ad libitum_ followed by 2 days of fasting. The 2 days could be either consecutive or separate days within a week. Although an individual could overeat during feasting days, there is a net deficit due to the alternating fasting days. However, it could be argued that adherence to and compliance with fasting days could be challenging to sustain and implement long-term.\n#### Time restricted eating\nInvestigators tried various timed feeding to optimize the timing for maximal health outcome benefits. The clinical trials included early TRF from 8 AM to 5 PM, mid-TRE ending at 7 PM, or late TRE beginning at 12 PM. In a robust supervised randomized controlled crossover feeding study, Sutton et al. showed that timed feeding for 6 h (8 AM−2 PM) with the intake of 33% of the individual's daily energy requirements containing 50% carbohydrates (3 meals/day), improved insulin sensitivity, beta cell functions, and metabolic health, compared to the control group which was allowed to consume the same calories (33% daily energy requirements, 50% carbohydrates) for 12 h (8 AM-8 PM) tailored to the individual preferences ([43](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9650338/#B43)). However, the study was conducted for 5 weeks in only 8 individuals who completed the study from 938 assessed for initial eligibility due to difficulty in recruitment for a supervised eating study. Similar findings were obtained from other short-term studies ([26](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9650338/#B26), [47](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9650338/#B47)"}, {"url": "https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2090123226002250", "title": "Chrono-nutrition in precision nutrition: Integrating chronotype into ...", "content": "Skip to main content Skip to article\n\n- View  PDF\n\nSearch ScienceDirect\n\n\n\n## Journal of Advanced Research Available online 11 March 2026\n\nIn Press, Corrected Proof What’s this? A., Roth, G. A. & Fuster, V. The Global Burden of Cardiovascular Diseases and Risk Factors 2020 and Beyond. J. Am. College Cardiol. 74, 2529–2532 (2019).\"). Healthy dietary habits may present an effective strategy to protect the vascular endothelium. As such, the Mediterranean diet (MD; characterized by a high portion of cereals, legumes, vegetables, and fruits, a moderate amount of protein from fish and meat, and fat, especially from olive oil), has been associated with multiple benefits for the prevention of CVD and other diseases[2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44324-023-00002-1#ref-CR2 \"Esposito, K. et al. A journey into a Mediterranean diet and type 2 diabetes: a systematic review with meta-analyses. BMJ Open 5, e008222 (2015).\"),[3](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44324-023-00002-1#ref-CR3 \"Sleiman, D., Al-Badri, M. R. & Azar, S. T. Effect of mediterranean diet in diabetes control and cardiovascular risk modification: a systematic review. Front Pub. Health 3, 69 (2015).\"),[4](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44324-023-00002-1#ref-CR4 \"Fito, M. et al. Effect of a traditional Mediterranean diet on lipoprotein oxidation: a randomized controlled trial. Arch Intern. Med. 167, 1195–1203 (2007).\"),[5](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44324-023-00002-1#ref-CR5 \"Tripp, M. L. et al. A Low-Glycemic, Mediterranean Diet and Lifestyle Modification Program with Targeted Nutraceuticals Reduces Body Weight, Improves Cardiometabolic Variables and Longevity Biomarkers in Overweight Subjects: A 13-Week Observational Trial. J. Med. Food 22, 479–489 (2019).\"). MD reduces the incidence of coronary heart disease, stroke, major fatal and non-fatal CVD events[6](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44324-023-00002-1#ref-CR6 \"Stewart, R. A. H. Primary Prevention of Cardiovascular Disease with a Mediterranean Diet Supplemented with Extra-Virgin Olive Oil or Nuts. N Engl J. Med. 379, 1388 (2018).\"),[7](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44324-023-00002-1#ref-CR7 \"Stefler, D. et al. Mediterranean diet score and total and cardiovascular mortality in Eastern Europe: the HAPIEE study. Eur. J. Nutr. 56, 421–429 (2017).\"),[8](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44324-023-00002-1#ref-CR8 \"Sofi, F., Cesari, F., Abbate, R., Gensini, G. F. & Casini, A. Adherence to Mediterranean diet and health status: meta-analysis. BMJ. 337, a1344 (2008).\"),[9](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44324-023-00002-1#ref-CR9 \"Becerra-Tomas, N. et al. Mediterranean diet, cardiovascular disease and mortality in diabetes: A systematic review and meta-analysis of prospective cohort studies and randomized clinical trials. Crit Rev. Food Sci. Nutr 60, 1207–1227 (2020).\"), and all-cause mortality[5](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44324-023-00002-1#ref-CR5 \"Tripp, M. L. et al. The Mediterranean-style dietary pattern and mortality among men and women with cardiovascular disease. Am. J. Clin. Nutr. 99, 172–180 (2014).\"), as well as hypertension[11](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44324-023-00002-1#ref-CR11 \"Magriplis, E. et al. Presence of Hypertension Is Reduced by Mediterranean Diet Adherence in All Individuals with a More Pronounced Effect in the Obese: The Hellenic National Nutrition and Health Survey (HNNHS). Nutrients. 12, 853 (2020).\"), improves antioxidant capacity[12](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44324-023-00002-1#ref-CR12 \"Ortner Hadziabdic, M., Vitali Cepo, D., Rahelic, D. & Bozikov, V. The Effect of the Mediterranean Diet on Serum Total Antioxidant Capacity in Obese Patients: A Randomized Controlled Trial. J. Am. Coll. Nutr. 35, 224–235 (2016).\"), and decreases total cholesterol[5](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44324-023-00002-1#ref-CR5 \"Tripp, M. L. et al. A Low-Glycemic, Mediterranean Diet and Lifestyle Modification Program with Targeted Nutraceuticals Reduces Body Weight, Improves Cardiometabolic Variables and Longevity Biomarkers in Overweight Subjects: A 13-Week Observational Trial. J. Med. Food 22, 479–489 (2019).\"),[13](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44324-023-00002-1#ref-CR13 \"Estruch, R. et al. Effects of a Mediterranean-style diet on cardiovascular risk factors: a randomized trial. Ann. Intern. Med. 145, 1–11 (2006).\"), oxidized-low density lipoprotein (OxLDL)[4](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44324-023-00002-1#ref-CR4 \"Fito, M. et al. Effect of a traditional Mediterranean diet on lipoprotein oxidation: a randomized controlled trial. Arch Intern. Med. 167, 1195–1203 (2007).\"),[5](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44324-023-00002-1#ref-CR5 \"Tripp, M. L. et al. J. Med. Food 22, 479–489 (2019).\"), C-reactive protein[5](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44324-023-00002-1#ref-CR5 \"Tripp, M. L. et al. A Low-Glycemic, Mediterranean Diet and Lifestyle Modification Program with Targeted Nutraceuticals Reduces Body Weight, Improves Cardiometabolic Variables and Longevity Biomarkers in Overweight Subjects: A 13-Week Observational Trial. J. Med. Food 22, 479–489 (2019).\"),[13](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44324-023-00002-1#ref-CR13 \"Estruch, R. et al. Effects of a Mediterranean-style diet on cardiovascular risk factors: a randomized trial. Ann. Intern. Med. 145, 1–11 (2006).\"), and triglycerides[5](https://www.nature.com/articles/s44324-023-00002-1#ref-CR5 \"Tripp, M. L. et al.
